title: Spinning for weaving: warp yarn
subtitle: What makes a handspun yarn suitable as a weaving warp, and how to spin one that survives the tension and abrasion of the loom
excerpt: Warp yarn needs different properties from knitting or crochet yarn: higher twist, greater abrasion resistance, and consistent thickness. This reading explains what a warp yarn requires and how to adjust spinning technique to produce yarn that holds up across a full warp.
sourceNotes: Warp-yarn spinning requirements synthesised from general handweaving and spinning references, including the principles in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910, public domain).

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads tensioned lengthways on the loom through which the weft passes on each pick.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp, interlacing with the warp threads on each row.
The number of complete twists in one inch of yarn, measured by counting twists across a marked length. Higher twist per inch makes a stronger, more abrasion-resistant yarn.
A plied yarn in which the twist of the singles and the ply twist exactly cancel each other out, producing a yarn that hangs without bias and lies flat when woven.
The number of warp threads per unit of width. The sett determines how tightly the warp is packed and must be matched to the yarn diameter.

content.text: The most common mistake when using handspun yarn as a
warp

is spinning yarn that is too soft and low-twist. Warp yarn sits under continuous tension and is abraded by the reed and the heddles on every shed change. It needs to be firmer, more tightly twisted, and more consistent in diameter than yarn made for
weft
, knitting, or crochet. This is not a limitation but a design parameter: warp yarn plays a structural role, and its properties are as important to the cloth as the weave structure itself.
What makes a good warp yarn
Higher twist
A yarn spun at a higher
twist per inch

is more resistant to abrasion because the fibres are bound together more firmly along the yarn axis. For most medium-weight warp yarn, aim for 12-15 twists per inch in the singles and a balanced ply at a similar count. Counting twist per inch on a sample is simple: mark 2.5 cm on a length of singles, then count how many times the yarn untwists as you hold one end fixed and the other free. Multiply the count to give twists per inch (one full untwist is one twist).
Consistent diameter
Inconsistent diameter in warp yarn creates uneven
sett

within a dent (thin sections of yarn sit at a looser set than thick sections, producing warp-way streaks in the finished cloth). Spin from a consistent, well-prepared fibre source: combed top produces more even singles than a carded rolag for warp applications. Practise drafting at a consistent rate.
Balanced ply
A
balanced ply

hangs straight without twist bias and lies flat when woven. An unbalanced ply twists slightly in the cloth and can cause the finished weaving to lean to one side, particularly in plain weave. Check balance by cutting a 30 cm length and holding it at both ends 15 cm apart: a balanced yarn hangs without any tendency to kink or twist.
Fibre choice

Longwool breeds (Bluefaced Leicester, Wensleydale, Lincoln) make excellent warp yarn. Their long fibres give natural abrasion resistance without needing extra twist. Merino and other fine-staple wools work as warp but need more twist to compensate for the shorter fibre length. Cotton is a good warp fibre but spins very differently: it has no elasticity and needs a worsted-style draw.
Spinning the warp

Start with a combed preparation of a longwool breed. Set the wheel to a higher twist ratio than you would use for general-purpose yarn. Use a short worsted-style draw, pulling back against the twist rather than allowing it into the drafting zone. The singles should feel firm and slightly over-twisted compared to what you would use for knitting. Wind onto the niddy-noddy and measure a test length for twist per inch before committing to a full warp.
Testing the finished yarn

Cut a 30 cm length of the plied yarn and rub it rapidly against a piece of card 20 times. Good warp yarn shows minimal pilling or fluffing on the surface. Hold both ends of the length and pull firmly: the yarn should not break. Pass the length through a reed dent ten times while pulling it under slight tension: it should slide through without abrading. If it pills or breaks, increase the twist or switch to a longer-staple fibre.

items.problem: Warp threads break repeatedly at the reed during weaving
items.cause: The twist is too low or the sett is too close for the yarn diameter
items.fix: Check the twist per inch against the benchmark for the yarn weight. Also check the sett: an oversett warp creates friction that breaks even good warp yarn. Reduce the sett by one dent per 5 cm and see if breakage stops.

items.problem: The warp threads felt together in the shed during weaving
items.cause: Fine merino singles with very high twist can felt against each other under the combination of heat, moisture from damp hands, and friction
items.fix: Size the warp (apply a thin paste of rice water or cornstarch dissolved in water) before dressing the loom. Warp sizing stiffens the surface and prevents felting.

items.problem: The finished cloth has warp-way streaks at regular intervals
items.cause: Thick and thin spots in the singles correspond to a looser or tighter set within the dent
items.fix: Inspect the warp yarn for thick-thin sections before warping. Even out the diameter in the next spinning session by practising consistent drafting speed.
